analogy: Think of "Minerals and Rocks" through the chapter's own opening images: LANDFORMS This unit deals with • Landforms and their evolution • Geomorphic processes — weathering, mass wasting, erosion and deposition; soils — formation UNIT III GEOMORPHIC PROCESSES C H A P T E R After learning about how the earth was born, how it evolved... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Minerals and Rocks is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that lANDFORMS This unit deals with • Landforms and their evolution • Geomorphic processes — weathering, mass wasting, erosion and deposition; soils — formation UNIT III

The text explains that gEOMORPHIC PROCESSES C H A P T E R After learning about how the earth was born, how it evolved its crust and other inner layers, how its crustal plates moved and are moving, and other information on earthquakes, the forms of volcanism and about the rocks and minerals the crust is composed of, it is time to know in detail about the surface of the earth on which we live.

The text explains that you are well aware that it has moved and moves vertically and horizontally.

The text explains that of course, it moved a bit faster in the past than the rate at which it is moving now.

The text explains that the differences in the internal forces operating from within the earth which built up the crust have been responsible for the variations in the outer surface of the crust.

The text explains that the earth’s surface is being continuously subjected to external forces induced basically by energy (sunlight).

The text explains that of course, the internal forces are still active though with different intensities.

The text explains that that means, the earth’s surface is being continuously subjected to by external forces originating within the earth’s atmosphere and by internal forces from within the earth.

The text explains that the external forces are known as exogenic forces and the internal forces are known as endogenic forces.

The text explains that the actions of exogenic forces result in wearing down (degradation) of relief/elevations and filling up (aggradation) of basins/ depressions, on the earth’s surface.

The text explains that the phenomenon of wearing down of relief variations of the surface of the earth through erosion is known as gradation.

The text explains that the endogenic forces continuously elevate or build up parts of the earth’s surface and hence the exogenic processes fail to even out the relief variations of the surface of the earth.

The text explains that so, variations remain as long as the opposing actions of exogenic and endogenic forces continue.

The text explains that in general terms, the endogenic forces are mainly land building forces and the exogenic processes are mainly land wearing forces.

The text explains that humans depend on it for their sustenance and have been using it extensively and intensively.

The text explains that so, it is essential to understand its nature in order to use it effectively without disturbing its balance and diminishing its potential for the future.

The text explains that however, humans have caused extensive damage to the environment through over use of resources.

The text explains that diastrophism and volcanism are endogenic geomorphic processes.

The text explains that an agent is a mobile medium (like running water, moving ice masses, wind, waves and currents etc.) which removes, transports and deposits earth materials.

The text explains that running water, groundwater, glaciers, wind, waves and currents, etc., can be called geomorphic agents.

The text explains that gravity besides being a directional force activating all downslope movements of matter also causes stresses on the earth’s materials.

The text explains that indirect gravitational stresses activate wave and tide induced currents and winds.

The text explains that all the movements either within the earth or on the surface of the earth occur due to gradients — from higher levels to lower levels, from high pressure to low pressure areas etc.

The text explains that this energy due to geothermal gradients and heat flow from within induces diastrophism and volcanism in the lithosphere.

The text explains that due to variations in geothermal gradients and heat flow from within, crustal thickness and strength, the action of endogenic forces are not uniform and hence the tectonically controlled original crustal surface is uneven.

The text explains that diastrophism All processes that move, elevate or build up portions of the earth’s crust come under diastrophism.

The text explains that they include: (i) orogenic processes involving mountain building through severe folding and affecting long and narrow belts of the earth’s crust; (ii) epeirogenic processes involving uplift or warping of large parts of the earth’s crust; (iii) earthquakes involving local relatively minor movements; (iv) plate tectonics involving horizontal movements of crustal plates.

The text explains that in the process of orogeny, the crust is severely deformed into folds.

The text explains that through the processes of orogeny, epeirogeny, earthquakes and plate tectonics, there can be faulting and fracturing of the crust.

The text explains that all these processes cause pressure, volume and temperature (PVT) changes which in turn induce metamorphism of rocks.

The text explains that volcanism Volcanism includes the movement of molten rock (magma) onto or toward the earth’s surface and also formation of many intrusive and extrusive volcanic forms.

The text explains that many aspects of volcanism have already been dealt in detail

The text explains that the density, type and distribution of vegetation which largely depend upon precipitation and temperature also exert under volcanoes in the Unit II and under igneous rocks in the preceding chapter in this unit.

The text explains that what do the words volcanism and volcanoes indicate?

The text explains that gravitational force acts upon all earth materials having a sloping surface and tend to produce movement of matter in down slope direction.

The text explains that it is this stress that breaks rocks and other earth materials.

The text explains that besides the gravitational stress earth materials become subjected to molecular stresses that may be caused by a number of factors amongst which temperature changes, crystallisation and melting are the most common.

The text explains that chemical processes normally lead to loosening of bonds between grains, dissolving of soluble minerals or cementing materials.

The text explains that temperature and precipitation are the two important climatic elements that control various processes.

The text explains that the flow chart (Figure 5.1) gives the denudation processes and their respective influence indirectly on exogenic geomorphic processes.

The text explains that further, due to differences in wind velocities and directions, amount and kind of precipitation, its intensity, the relation between precipitation and evaporation, daily range of temperature, freezing and thawing frequency, depth of frost penetration, the geomorphic processes vary within any climatic region.

The text explains that climatic factors being equal, the intensity of action of exogenic geomorphic processes depends upon type and structure of rocks.

The text explains that the term structure includes such aspects of rocks as folds, faults, orientation and inclination of beds, presence or absence of joints, bedding planes, hardness or softness of constituent minerals, chemical susceptibility of mineral constituents; the permeability or impermeability Figure 5.1 : Denudational processes and their driving forces

The text explains that different types of rocks with differences in their structure offer varying resistances to various geomorphic processes.

The text explains that a particular rock may be resistant to one process and non- resistant to another.

The text explains that and, under varying climatic conditions, particular rocks may exhibit different degrees of resistance to geomorphic processes and hence they operate at differential rates and give rise to differences in topography.

The text explains that the effects of most of the exogenic geomorphic processes are small and slow and may be imperceptible in a short time span, but will in the long run affect the rocks severely due to continued fatigue.

The text explains that finally, it boils down to one fact that the differences on the surface of the earth though originally related to the crustal evolution continue to exist in some form or the other due to differences in the type and structure of earth materials, differences in geomorphic processes and in their rates of operation.

The text explains that weathering is defined as mechanical disintegration and chemical decom- position of rocks through the actions of various elements of weather and climate.

The text explains that not only weathering processes differ from climate to climate, but also the depth of the weathering mantle (Figure 5.2).

The text explains that (a) Granite (c) Quartz (b) Clay (d) Salts (iii) Debris avalanche can be included in the category of: (a) Landslides (c) Rapid flow mass movements (b) Slow flow mass movements (d) Subsidence 2.

The text explains that (i) It is weathering that is responsible for bio-diversity on the earth.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 5: Minerals and Rocks

prelims_traps: Do not treat LANDFORMS and GEOMORPHIC PROCESSES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Minerals and Rocks; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Minerals and Rocks through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LANDFORMS and GEOMORPHIC PROCESSES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
